examples.title: Algorithm: Mechanism vs Policy Separation
examples.description: Demonstrates how the same mechanism can support different policies, illustrating the key OS design principle of separating mechanism from policy
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CPU_Scheduler_Mechanism
INPUT: Current process, time quantum
OUTPUT: Next process to execute

BEGIN
 // MECHANISM: Timer interrupt for preemption
 SET timer_interrupt = TRUE
 WHEN timer_interrupt OCCURS:
 SAVE current_process_state
 CALL Scheduler_Policy(next_policy)
 RESTORE next_process_state
 DISPATCH next_process

ALGORITHM Scheduler_Policy
INPUT: policy_type (FCFS, ROUND_ROBIN, PRIORITY)
OUTPUT: next_process

BEGIN
 SWITCH policy_type OF
 CASE FCFS:
 next_process = SELECT first_process_in_queue
 CASE ROUND_ROBIN:
 next_process = SELECT next_process_round_robin
 CASE PRIORITY:
 next_process = SELECT highest_priority_process
 RETURN next_process

// KEY INSIGHT: Same timer mechanism (MECHANISM) can work with
// any scheduling algorithm (POLICY). Changing policy does not
// require changing the timer interrupt code.
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the core principle of separating mechanism from policy in OS design. The timer interrupt mechanism (HOW CPU is preempted) is separate from the scheduling algorithm (WHAT process runs next). This allows the OS to support different scheduling policies (FCFS, Round Robin, Priority) without modifying the underlying preemption mechanism.

examples.title: Python: Demonstrating Mechanism-Policy Separation
examples.description: Shows how the same scheduling mechanism can support different scheduling policies
examples.code:
"""
Operating System Design Principle: Mechanism vs Policy Separation
This demonstrates how the SAME mechanism can support DIFFERENT policies.
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Optional
import time

# Data structures for process representation
@dataclass
class Process:
 pid: int
 name: str
 priority: int # Lower number = higher priority
 burst_time: int
 remaining_time: int = 0
 
 def __post_init__(self):
 self.remaining_time = self.burst_time


# ============================================================
# MECHANISM: This is the FIXED part - the scheduling framework
# ============================================================
class CPUSchedulerMechanism:
 """
 MECHANISM: The underlying scheduling framework that doesn't change
 regardless of which scheduling policy is used.
 """
 def __init__(self, policy: 'SchedulingPolicy'):
 self.policy = policy
 self.ready_queue: List[Process] = []
 self.current_time = 0
 self.completed_processes: List[Process] = []
 
 def add_process(self, process: Process):
 """Add process to ready queue - mechanism doesn't care about policy"""
 self.ready_queue.append(process)
 print(f"[MECHANISM] Process {process.pid} added to ready queue")
 
 def dispatch_next(self) -> Optional[Process]:
 """
 MECHANISM: This is FIXED - it calls the policy to select next process
 The mechanism doesn't know or care WHICH policy is being used
 """
 if not self.ready_queue:
 return None
 
 # MECHANISM: Ask POLICY which process to run next
 # This is the key separation point!
 next_process = self.policy.select_next_process(self.ready_queue)
 
 if next_process:
 self.ready_queue.remove(next_process)
 print(f"[MECHANISM] Dispatching process {next_process.pid} using {self.policy.policy_name} policy")
 
 return next_process
 
 def run_simulation(self):
 """Run the scheduling simulation"""
 print(f"\n=== Running Scheduler with {self.policy.policy_name} Policy ===\n")
 
 while self.ready_queue:
 process = self.dispatch_next()
 if process:
 # Simulate process execution
 time.sleep(0.1) # Small delay for demonstration
 process.remaining_time -= 1
 self.current_time += 1
 
 if process.remaining_time > 0:
 # Put back in queue (for demonstration)
 self.ready_queue.append(process)
 else:
 self.completed_processes.append(process)
 print(f"[MECHANISM] Process {process.pid} completed\n")


# ============================================================
# POLICIES: These can be CHANGED without touching the mechanism
# ============================================================
class SchedulingPolicy(ABC):
 """Abstract base class for scheduling policies"""
 
 @abstractmethod
 def select_next_process(self, queue: List[Process]) -> Optional[Process]:
 """Select the next process to run - THIS IS THE POLICY"""
 pass


class FCFSPolicy(SchedulingPolicy):
 """POLICY: First Come First Served"""
 policy_name = "FCFS"
 
 def select_next_process(self, queue: List[Process]) -> Optional[Process]:
 """Select first process that arrived (FIFO)"""
 return queue[0] if queue else None


class PriorityPolicy(SchedulingPolicy):
 """POLICY: Priority Scheduling (lower number = higher priority)"""
 policy_name = "Priority"
 
 def select_next_process(self, queue: List[Process]) -> Optional[Process]:
 """Select process with highest priority (lowest number)"""
 return min(queue, key=lambda p: p.priority) if queue else None


class RoundRobinPolicy(SchedulingPolicy):
 """POLICY: Round Robin Scheduling"""
 policy_name = "Round Robin"
 
 def select_next_process(self, queue: List[Process]) -> Optional[Process]:
 """Select next process in circular order"""
 return queue[0] if queue else None


# ============================================================
# DEMONSTRATION
# ============================================================
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create sample processes
 processes = [
 Process(1, "Chrome", 3, 5),
 Process(2, "VS Code", 1, 3), # Highest priority
 Process(3, "Spotify", 2, 4),
 ]
 
 # DEMONSTRATION 1: Use FCFS Policy
 print("=" * 60)
 print("DEMO 1: Using FCFS Policy")
 print("=" * 60)
 scheduler = CPUSchedulerMechanism(FCFSPolicy())
 for p in processes:
 scheduler.add_process(p)
 # Note: Not running full simulation to keep output readable
 
 # DEMONSTRATION 2: Use Priority Policy (same mechanism!)
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("DEMO 2: Using Priority Policy (SAME MECHANISM)")
 print("=" * 60)
 scheduler2 = CPUSchedulerMechanism(PriorityPolicy())
 for p in processes:
 scheduler2.add_process(p)
 
 print("\n[KEY INSIGHT]")
 print("Notice: We used the SAME CPUSchedulerMechanism class")
 print("but simply passed a different policy object!")
 print("This is the power of separating mechanism from policy.")
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the mechanism-policy separation principle. The CPUSchedulerMechanism class represents the MECHANISM - the fixed framework for adding and dispatching processes. The SchedulingPolicy classes (FCFS, Priority, RoundRobin) represent the POLICIES - the decision rules for which process to run next. By passing different policy objects to the same mechanism, we can change scheduling behavior without modifying the underlying code. This illustrates the key OS design principle that makes systems flexible and maintainable.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for FCFS, O(n log n) for Priority (due to min operation)

examples.title: C: OS Implementation with Mechanism-Policy Separation
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating the mechanism-policy separation in a simple CPU scheduler
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the mechanism-policy separation using function pointers. The Scheduler structure contains a PolicySelector function pointer that points to different policy functions. The dispatch() function is the mechanism - it doesn't know or care which policy is being used; it simply calls the policy function. This is exactly how real operating systems implement flexibility: the kernel provides mechanisms (timer interrupts, context switching), and different policies (scheduling algorithms) can be loaded or changed without modifying the kernel code. The output shows how the same scheduler mechanism produces different execution orders depending on which policy function is plugged in.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for FCFS, O(n) for Priority (scans entire queue), O(n) for SJF

examples.title: Java: Demonstrating OS Portability Principles
examples.description: Shows how high-level language code achieves portability, a key advantage discussed in OS implementation
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates the portability advantages of using high-level languages for OS implementation. The code shows key OS components (ProcessControlBlock, MemoryManager, CPUScheduler) that would exist in any operating system. The critical point is that this SAME Java code (or C code) can be compiled and run on different hardware architectures (x86, ARM, RISC-V, etc.) without modification. This is exactly why Dennis Ritchie rewrote UNIX in C in 1973 - it made the OS portable. The code demonstrates how OS components abstract hardware details, providing a consistent interface regardless of the underlying processor architecture.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for memory allocation, O(n) for context switching simulation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for process storage, O(1) per process for registers
